data.title: The Optimist Fund Q4 2025 Quarterly Letter
data.description:
To the Partners of Optimist Fund,

When we launched Optimist in 2022, our objective was explicit. Deliver exceptional long term investment performance. We articulate this as compounding capital at mid teens or better over decades. That goal remains unchanged and it is what we wake up every morning working toward.

Optimist has been operating for just under four years. While we have not yet completed our first five-year vintage, an important point given that we invest with a five-year lens, we are tracking in a direction I am pleased with.

% Returns Optimist Fund* Benchmark**
2025 32.2% 8.9%
Q4 2025 -8.5% -2.0%
Q3 2025 9.1% 7.9%
Q2 2025 39.2% 11.3%
Q1 2025 -4.9% -7.5%
2024 66.5% 34.1%
2023 82.9% 27.4%
2022*** -51.4% -11.8%
Compound Returns as of December 31st, 2025
1 Year 32.2% 8.9%
2 Year 48.4% 20.8%
3 Year 59.1% 23.0%
Since Inception 19.1% 13.8%
***Fund start date was March 1, 2022.

In Q4, Optimist declined 8.5 percent. While short-term drawdowns are never enjoyable, we remain highly encouraged by the fundamental performance of the businesses we own and have strong conviction in our prospective returns over the next five years.

Before walking through Q4 attribution and our positioning for 2026, I want to address an important higher-level question that I know sits in the minds of some investors.

1. Why have we been able to deliver market leading performance over the past four years?

2. Why do we believe that performance is repeatable over the next five and beyond?

Why We've Outperformed

We're Focused

At the core of our results is a very specific focus. Identifying businesses where deep, targeted research can uncover a meaningful gap between market expectations and long-term reality. We refer to this as developing unique insights.

If the market believes a company will generate five billion dollars of profit in five years and values it accordingly, but our research suggests it is far more likely to earn ten billion dollars over that same period, the outcome, if we are right, tends to be strong investment returns.

The obvious follow up question is how we consistently identify what the market is missing.

We do this by focusing on specific types of companies which can be overlooked or misunderstood for specific reasons we have come to understand.

We refer to these opportunities as Compounders in Disguise and Inflecting Compounders, which we discussed in depth at our Investor Day and in our Q2 2025 Investor Letter. By deliberately focusing on these specific types of businesses, where we have developed deep domain expertise, we increase the likelihood of uncovering differentiated insight and, in turn, opportunities to materially outperform over the long term. This discipline, knowing where we can have an edge and ignoring the rest, is a core reason we believe our results have been strong and, more importantly, repeatable.

We Play the Long Game

From day one, we have been explicit that Optimist is run to maximize five-year investment outcomes, not quarterly results. As a result, stock price volatility is not something we fear. In fact, it is often an amplifier of long-term performance.

The bear markets of 2022 and 2025 allowed us to rebalance aggressively into businesses whose long-term fundamentals remained intact, but whose share prices were indiscriminately punished.

Being comfortable with short term volatility is a requirement to successfully execute our investment approach and to continue delivering the type of returns we have in the past.

Over the last four years, executing against this approach led us to add meaningfully to positions such as Carvana (CVNA), Wayfair (W), ThredUp (TDUP), DoorDash (DASH), and Uber (UBER) during periods of extreme share price weakness. Those decisions have been very profitable. It has also led us to add to positions such as HelloFresh (HLFFF) and Peloton (PTON), which were not good investments.

This brings me to an important final point as to why we have been able to generate strong long-term results.

We Acknowledge and Accept That We Will Not Get Everything Right

And importantly, we do not need to. The capital generated from our collection of correct investment decisions more than offsets inevitable losses from our mistakes, leaving us with attractive long-term investment returns.

Why We Believe Outperformance Is Repeatable

Many investors deliver strong early results by being a big fish in a small pond, only to lose their edge as assets grow and their opportunity set shrinks. Though our assets under management have increased over ten-fold over the last three years, our strategy can scale to at least 20x our current AUM.

The average market capitalization of our holdings has always exceeded five billion dollars and today sits above thirty billion dollars USD. Our edge does not depend on exploiting microcap or small cap inefficiencies. It comes from differentiated fundamental insight in global category leading businesses whose market capitalizations are most often measured in the many billions.

Furthermore, our results have not been driven by riding thematic cycles, whether commodities, AI, or broader technology waves. Our outperformance has come from idiosyncratic, company specific insights that are largely independent of macro or sector rotations.

Optimist launched in 2022, one of the most challenging periods for equity investors, particularly for those focused on higher-growth companies. Under significant pressure, many managers capitulated and exited businesses such as Carvana, DoorDash, Uber, Netflix (NFLX), and Meta (META) after share prices declined more than fifty percent at their lows. Instead, they rotated into companies perceived as safer simply because their share prices had fallen less. That decision proved costly, as many of those same businesses have meaningfully outperformed since the end of 2022.

As a result, those managers underperformed on the way down and again on the way up.

Our results were not the product of fortunate timing. They were the result of remaining disciplined to our investment philosophy when doing so was most difficult.

Stepping back, it should be clear that our edge is not accidental or dependent on a fleeting market condition. We know what we are good at. We are good at identifying misunderstood compounders, developing differentiated insight through deep research, and acting decisively when volatility creates opportunity. That skill set is not transitory. It is rooted in process, temperament, and focus.

Sustained long-term outperformance will never simply happen on its own. It requires continued intellectual honesty, relentless work, and a willingness to be uncomfortable and unpopular when the data supports it. We are well equipped for that challenge. More importantly, we are energized by it.

Looking Ahead

We believe the next five years will be better than the last four.

Not because of a top-down positive macro economic view but because current valuations, in our view, materially underappreciate the earnings and cash flow our core holdings will generate five years from now.

That gap is measurable. Consensus expectations remain anchored to prior periods, while the underlying businesses have materially improved. Cost structures are leaner, balance sheets are stronger, unit economics are clearer and sales and earnings growth are accelerating.

We are seeing this in real-time.

Optimist Fund's best days are ahead.

Q4 Review

Top Q4 Contributors Contribution Top Q4 Detractors Contribution
Wayfair 1.5% ThredUp -7.1%
Carvana 1.5% Monday.com (MNDY) -1.4%
Latham -1.0%
Top Contributor Total 3.0% Top Detractor Total -9.5%

Top Contributors

Wayfair – Last quarter, we highlighted that Wayfair's Q2 results were the strongest we had seen in several years and supported our thesis that the business was positioned for a meaningful rebound as the home furnishings market recovered. Q3 built on that momentum. Revenue grew 9% year over year, accelerating from 6% in Q2, while adjusted EBITDA increased more than 70% year over year. These results provide further evidence that Wayfair's future profit growth remains substantial and is materially underappreciated by the market. Wayfair continues to be a top five holding.

Carvana – Carvana's strong financial trajectory continues reporting record third-quarter results, with 155,941 retail cars sold (+~44% YoY) and total revenue of ~$5.65 billion (+~55% YoY), both all-time quarterly highs. The company generated adjusted EBITDA of ~$637 million +~50%, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of ~11.3%, well above typical industry profitability levels. Carvana remains a top 5 holding.

Top Detractors

ThredUp – ThredUp delivered an excellent third quarter, with revenue up approximately 34% year over year, one of the fastest growth rates in its recent history. Adjusted EBITDA improved to $3.8 million, or a 4.6% margin, from roughly breakeven last year, while active buyers increased about 26%, driven by strong new buyer acquisition. Management also raised full year revenue guidance, signaling confidence in the outlook.

Notably, management highlighted that new buyer growth accelerated to 81% year over year in October, up from 54% in Q3, following a rebrand and the launch of a new product recommendation feed in late September.

While fundamentals continue to strengthen, the stock has declined over the past five months. These dislocations between price and fundamentals are common in the types of businesses we invest in and have led us to add to the position. ThredUp remains our largest holding.

Monday.com – In November, Monday.com reported a mixed quarter, with revenue and free cash flow growing approximately 26% and 10% year over year, respectively. Enterprise performance continued to accelerate, while growth in small and mid sized customers further decelerated.

We held the position through year end and into 2026, believing management could execute against the objectives they had outlined, supported by a strong historical track record. However, following the company's fourth quarter earnings release earlier this week, we have fully exited the position. The reported results were not aligned with our forecasts, and more importantly, management was unable to clearly articulate what is occurring within the business.

When we lose confidence in the accuracy or clarity of management's communication, we exit. That is what we have done here.

Latham Group (SWIM) – Latham delivered a solid third quarter, with net sales of approximately $162 million, up ~7.6% year-over-year, outperforming a generally flat U.S. in-ground pool market and reflecting continued traction across key product lines, including pool covers, liners, and fiberglass pools. Adjusted EBITDA itself grew roughly 28.5% to about $38.3 million. Latham also narrowed and modestly raised its full-year guidance, signaling confidence in its outlook despite broader industry headwinds, with net sales now expected in the ~$540–$550 million range and adjusted EBITDA of ~$92–$98 million. Overall, the quarter demonstrated a business that continues to grow revenue, expand profitability, and execute on strategic priorities even in challenging end-market conditions. We are pleased with results.

On the negative side, Latham's CEO, Scott Rajeski, announced his retirement. The incoming CEO appears credible, coming from James Hardie, a much larger organization, but we have not yet had the opportunity to engage with him and plan to do so following year-end results. Given the unexpected leadership change, we reduced the position from 6% to 3% of the fund. This was not driven by a change in our view of the business, but by our emphasis on management quality and familiarity. Until we build conviction in the new leadership, our overall conviction is lower.

Aggregate Portfolio Composition

Here are the top 10 holdings in our fund, comprising approximately 85% of the portfolio as of December 31 st, 2025.

ThredUp Uber
Carvana Monday.com
Wayfair Latham Group
First Advantage (FA) DiscoverIE (DCVNF)
DoorDash Root (ROOT)

Other Notable Changes

During the quarter, we exited our position in Fiverr (FVRR), which had previously been among our top ten holdings. We also initiated new positions in Root, a disruptive insurance company, and Affirm (AFRM), the leading buy now pay later provider in the United States. In addition, we increased our investment in DiscoverIE, elevating it to a top ten holding.

Our investment in Fiverr was based on the belief that the company could reaccelerate organic revenue growth back into the mid-teens, which we believed would drive a meaningful re-rating of the shares. Under that scenario, our five-year valuation target was well over $100 per share, compared to a share price of roughly $20 as of December 31st.

In Q4, we made the decision that we would rather own Fiverr at a higher price with greater conviction that their growth will reaccelerate than own it today at a lower price with less conviction. Put differently, we would be comfortable purchasing the shares at $30 if we had stronger evidence that revenue growth is sustainably accelerating and that our longer-term ~$100+ valuation target is increasingly likely to be realized.

This decision reflects our discipline around conviction and opportunity cost. While we do not own Fiverr today, it remains on our watchlist, and we would not hesitate to re-establish a position should the potential accelerating operating trends, begin to materialize.

DiscoverIE: A High-Quality Industrial Compounder at an Inflection Point

DiscoverIE Group plc is a leading designer and manufacturer of highly differentiated electronic components for mission critical industrial applications. The company specializes in bespoke, application specific products, including magnetics, sensors, power electronics, and connectivity solutions, that are designed into customer systems and typically remain in place for the life of the product. Examples include custom temperature sensors used in specialized medical devices and X ray sensors embedded in airport security scanners. These niche, high value components are small in cost but critical to system performance and reliability.

The strategy is simple and disciplined: operate in structurally growing end markets, focus on low-volume, high-complexity components that are difficult to switch, and compound growth through organic design wins and bolt-on acquisitions. This model has enabled 20%+ EPS and free cash flow per share growth over the past 10 years.

While challenging end markets led to negative organic growth in both fiscal 2024/fiscal 2025 and compressed the valuation from over 25x earnings to ~15x today, we believe the business is entering the early stages of a cyclical recovery. In our view, this supports the potential for ~20% EPS growth over the next five years alongside meaningful multiple expansion.

With aligned management, disciplined capital allocation, and a focused growth strategy, DiscoverIE fits squarely within the type of under-appreciated industrial technology compounder we seek to own.

Closing Remarks

I hope after reading this letter, you feel you have a better understanding of what we are doing at Optimist and why we remain so excited about the opportunity ahead.

Thank you to everyone who joined us at our 2025 Year in Review Webinar a few weeks ago. If you missed it, click here to watch the replay.

We deeply appreciate your continued trust and partnership. As always, please feel free to reach out with any questions.

Speak soon,

Jordan McNamee

Founder & Chief Investment Officer

data.title: Rates Spark: Quiz Time
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas and Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

Here's how we view the curve now and in the period ahead

Essentially, the 10yr yield has been up and down in the past few years, briefly above 4.5% at times, and below 4% more often, but by and large has not shown a tendency to radically change its averaging at or about 4.2%.

And that, by the way, is at or about the neutral tendency that we identify for the 10yr Treasury yield. In other words, if the funds rate was close to neutral and inflation close to neutral, we should expect the 10yr yield to be around neutral too.

Currently, we have a funds rate at 3.64%, just a tad tight versus neutrality. But the market is discounting a return to neutrality over the course of 2026, which helps to pitch the 2yr yield at 3.3%. The totality of circumstances on the front end generally smacks of neutrality.

And inflation? We saw that printing at or about 2.5% last week. And inflation expectations tend to be in that territory too (or slightly below for longer tenors). That's all consistent with this notion of neutrality when it comes to pitching where the 10yr yield should be.

What about bond supply, and the fiscal deficit? Two things. First, the market does not seem to care. Second, the effect on longer tenors is being curtailed through reliance on heavier short-tenor issuance. What about Kevin Warsh's ambition to reduce the balance sheet?

If true, it must mean the Fed holds less and the market then needs to hold more. That adds to the weight-of-supply story. There is an offset coming, however, from the 1 April loosening in the leverage ratio requirements for the large US banks, which should allow them to hold more Treasuries (should they choose to do so). Overall, the theme here is mixed to bearish, but far from determinative.

And what about the slowdown risk for the US economy? This is such an interesting debate. There is a significant expectation for relatively poor macro circumstances ahead. But not poor enough, it seems, to push the funds rate expectation structurally and materially below 3%.

But if that is true, why the predominant expectation for macro weakness? The market discount is telling us that things are quite nuanced. We're left with the impression that the 10yr can very easily hot-tail back to where it came from in the 4.2% area, unless we hear or know more.

Wednesday’s events and market view

After a slow start, the US data calendar is getting busier with the release of the durable goods orders, housing start numbers and industrial production data – the consensus is looking for steady numbers.

The FOMC minutes of the January meeting could be more interesting since the Fed displayed a more hawkish tinge. The minutes could reveal to what extent this was more aimed at stemming the dollar weakness at that point in time. The Fed’s Bowman is slated to speak at a supervision and regulation-themed event.

There are no releases of note from the eurozone with only France releasing final CPI data for January. Of more interest will be the speeches of the ECB's Villeroy, Cipollone and Schnabel – the latter speaking on sovereign debt levels.

In primary markets, Germany will sell €5.5bn in 10y Bunds while the US Treasury will sell US$16bn in new 20y bonds.

data.title: Nomura International Core Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

International equities, as represented by the MSCI All Country World Index ('ACWI') ex USA Index, continued to display solid performance through 4Q2025, with the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index (NET) extending gains by 5.1% as global investors increasingly allocate outside the US amid attractive valuations and supportive macro dynamics. Broad international stocks – including developed and emerging markets – have meaningfully outpaced US equities year to date, underpinned by a weaker US dollar and improving earnings expectations across key regions.

At the country level, parts of Europe (particularly the UK and Switzerland) were notable contributors, buoyed by export resilience. The technology-heavy North Asian markets also fared well except for China, which posted weaker economic data. South Korean equities continued to be lifted by domestic policy and governance reforms momentum and improving earnings visibility from tech demand, while the rest of emerging markets added breadth. Sectoral divergence was clear this quarter: financials and traditional cyclicals led, contrasting with underperformance by growth-oriented sectors including consumer discretionary and communication services. This amplified the value-led leadership theme across international markets.

Within the Fund

In 4Q25, Nomura International Core Equity Fund posted a positive total return but underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index (net).

The quarter was marked by strong outperformance of value over growth stocks, which was a headwind for the Fund's core approach. As regards stock selection, it proved challenging across industrials, consumer discretionary, and healthcare, with prior longtime outperformers selling as their growth leadership moderated. In industrials, the Fund's defense exposures and consumer credit rating agency retraced in favor of more cyclical names, including machinery, construction, and building products companies most notably in Japan. Many of these names have traded as artificial intelligence ('AI') derivatives due to business exposure to the AI supply chain, which raised their valuations. Within consumer discretionary, the Fund's exposure to internet platform companies was negative while its overweight to Swiss multinational healthcare companies detracted as these names saw earnings resiliency despite tariff concerns and high valuations.

The Fund has experienced two very challenging quarters in a row, which had led to a significant valuation reset for many of our top holdings. We believe this will set them up for future success as growth continues. While our convictions have not been reduced for most of these holdings, the Fund eliminated exposure to stocks in which fundamentals have changed. These included Nintendo Co. Ltd. (NTDOY), Haeleon PLC, Makita Corp. (MKTAY), Siemens AG (SIEGY), and Mondi PLC (MONDY) amid a combination of cost, macro pressures, or simply what we perceived to be rich valuations for moderate growth. In turn, we initiated several new positions, including Barclays PLC (BCS), Olympus Corp. (OCPNY), Embraer SA (ERJ), and argenx SE (ARGX).

Among individual stocks, notable outperformers were SK Hynix (Korea), Barclays PLC ('UK'), and Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA (BBVA) ('BBVA') (Spain):

  • SK Hynix (SKHHY), a market leader in memory semiconductors, notably High Bandwidth Memory ('HBM') essential for AI infrastructure, was buoyed by strong 3Q earnings driven by strong demand and rising prices. The market perception of memory semiconductors is moving beyond the boom-bust commodity trade to their key role in core AI infrastructure.
  • Barclays, one of the UK's largest banks, benefited from continued positive sentiment toward European financials and strong 3Q financial results that prompted upgraded performance targets and an accelerated share buyback program.
  • Shares of BBVA, a leading banking franchise across Spain and Latin America, rallied further, driven primarily by a renewed focus on significant shareholder returns (dividends and substantial share buybacks) following the failure of its bid for Banco Sabadell, along with strong operational performance in its key markets of Mexico and Spain.

Notable individual detractors were Sea Ltd. (SE) (Singapore), MercadoLibre (MELI) (Argentina), and Grab Holdings Ltd. (GRAB) All three have distinct yet related reasons tied to competitive pressures, profitability concerns, and valuations dynamics.

  • Shares of MercadoLibre ('MELI'), Latin America's leading ecommerce and digital financial services company, saw further profit-taking during the period after the strong run-up in recent years. Intensifying competition from Amazon's expanding logistics footprint and Sea's Shopee platform in core markets including Brazil has forced MELI into defensive pricing and higher spending. We remain confident in the growth and MELI's dominant regional moat.
  • Sea, a leading consumer internet company at the intersection of rising digital consumption and financial inclusion in Southeast Asia and select global markets, also saw its shares pull back on rising costs and margin pressure despite strong revenue growth across ecommerce (Shopee) and digital financial services. We maintain a positive view based on long-term structural growth drivers, which remain intact, in our view.
  • Grab Holdings, a leading Southeast Asia super-app integrating ride-hailing, food delivery, and other services including financial services, saw its shares decline during the period due to profit-taking and mixed earnings reactions despite strong underlying growth. The company remains a key holding on the back of strength in mobility, continued profitability improvements, and fintech monetization that we believe will continue to support fundamentals growth.

Outlook

The outlook for international markets remains compelling, in our view.

Starting with Europe, the economic outlook continues to be supported by moderate inflation, consumer resilience, and fiscal and infrastructure spending, with capital expenditure increases seen around the energy transition, defense, and technology sectors, which could bolster competitiveness in the long run. Geopolitical tensions have moderated but risks remain around diverging fiscal paths across the regions. We continue to see momentum for defense spending in Europe despite a dip in defense stocks in recent months amid Ukraine security talks. As we previously wrote, NATO leaders agreed to increase defense spending to 5% of gross domestic product ('GDP') (or $1.5 trillion in additional annual spending across NATO). Another area of exposure has been industrials amid tailwinds from Germany's €500 billion infrastructure program, which has the potential to buoy the domestic economy. Overall, earnings consensus growth is strong for 2026 as investors have turned more constructive, driven by attractive valuations and gradually improving macro conditions in Europe.

In Asia, with the exception of Japan, central banks have embraced a dovish stance, made possible by the US Federal Reserve embarking on its own easing cycle. This should support economic activity across the region. Geopolitical concerns have also abated, removing an overhang on markets, although flare-ups are possible; India remains a sticky point while a truce with China has been extended. While Chinese technology stocks have momentum, the economy has much less. We will continue to look for ideas but maintain our valuation discipline. The outlook for Japan is positive based on governance reforms, fiscal support, and the underlying AI-related tailwinds, but we are cautious about various export-oriented companies as the Bank of Japan normalizes rates, potentially reversing the trajectory of a weak yen.

Despite a challenging few months for the portfolio, it is important to remain grounded. We maintain our valuation discipline and continue to devote most of our time assessing companies' fundamentals. Our focus is on companies we believe have long-term compounding characteristics, unrecognized growth potential, and high-quality cyclicals. This should lead to a portfolio with a diverse set of exposures and risk characteristics, which primarily generates returns through stock selection as opposed to country or thematic exposure.

We believe international markets represent a differentiated opportunity set of leading companies in sectors less prominent than in the US. Despite outperformance in 2025, international markets have been ignored for many years and continue to present attractive opportunities related to their US counterparts, in our view. We believe there is a chance for momentum unwind in the US, with a growing domestic investors base looking beyond the country for the first time in a long while. We believe there is a real possibility for international markets to catch up to their US counterparts in the next few years.

data.title: Tesla: Time To Ditch The 'Someday Soon' Premium
data.description:
Introduction

Is there a company and stock more polarizing than Tesla, Inc. (TSLA)? The controversial nature of the company is primarily attributable to its CEO, Elon Musk. Bulls see a company led by one of the most innovative people of our time (or perhaps all time) and bet on his capability to continue to deliver extraordinary success. Bears see outlandish, unrealistic goals promised on impossibly optimistic time horizons. In this article, I aim to analyze TSLA realistically but also fairly by exploring the following questions:

  • How does the “Musk Premium” work? Is it still warranted?
  • What are realistic expectations for different revenue segments by 2030?
  • What is the stock worth under an optimistic but plausible scenario?
  • Should investors buy TSLA at current prices?

Ultimately I conclude that TSLA makes very little sense as an investment at current valuations. Although I have long been of the belief that Elon Musk is worth investing in because of his tremendous successes in multiple different domains, there has to be a very strong potential ROI for me to invest given the risk of the stock. At a $1.5 trillion valuation, I don’t see a plausible roadmap for Tesla to return value to shareholders buying in at that price. Even applying the most optimistic set of assumptions I can envision, I only arrive at a price target of $301 per share for TSLA. Therefore, I rate the stock a strong sell.

Should There Still Be a “Musk Premium”?

Elon Musk’s companies have always been expensive because of Elon Musk. Investors want to be involved in his projects. I believe this is fueled in part by a psychological phenomenon called “FOMO,” or “Fear of Missing Out.” In my view, Musk monetizes this fear by selling a vision of the future where his products are both ubiquitous and life-changing. Notice that Musk’s companies don’t tend to be valued based on what they’re doing right now, but rather on what they will be doing in Musk’s promised future.

Tesla is not just a company that makes electric cars; it’s a company that is going to solve autonomous driving and put humanoid robots in factories and homes. SpaceX (SPACE) is not just a company that sends satellites into orbit; it’s the company that is going to create human settlements on the Moon and Mars, colonize space, and build data centers in orbit (how do we fix them if they break though?). xAI (X.AI) is not just a company that runs a social media platform and develops a chatbot and generative AI tools but is the company that is going to create AGI, ensure the end of work as we know it, and usher in the age of universal high income and paid leisure. Then you have Neuralink, which will merge man and machine, and the Boring Company, which will end the problem of traffic congestion.

But as has often been pointed out, Musk’s promises tend to either not materialize at all or take much, much longer to materialize than his forecasts suggest. As an example, here is what Elon Musk said in the July 23rd, 2025, TSLA earnings call for Q2:

And I think we'll probably have autonomous ride-hailing in probably half the population of the U.S. by the end of the year. That's at least our goal, subject to regulatory approvals. I think we'll technically be able to do it. So assuming we get regulatory approvals, it's probably addressing half of the population in the U.S. by the end of the year.

So far, robotaxi is only operating in Austin, Texas, according to Tesla's website. Musk’s history of outlandish promises goes back decades. In December 2015, Musk said, “We’re going to end up with complete autonomy…and I think we will have complete autonomy in approximately two years.” In June 2016, he called autonomous driving a “basically solved problem.” Here we are nearly ten years later, and by no objective metrics is it a solved problem.

Musk’s other promises, like the Hyperloop/Boring Company, cutting trillions in government spending, and recently Mars colonization (he tweeted that Mars is now on the back burner and that building a Moon base is the primary plan), have also not panned out. For a more comprehensive breakdown of Musk’s history of promises, I suggest checking out this article.

I want to be as generous as possible and assume that Elon Musk's overpromising is a result of his own exuberance and optimism. Functionally, though, I believe investors' faith in these promises continues to inflate TSLA's valuation. When a promise fails to pan out, it either gets doubled down on or replaced with an even grander promise. So what Elon Musk really sells is a vision of the future. His wealth is predicated not on how many cars Tesla sells or how many people subscribe to Starlink (STRLK), but on how many people want to hold stakes in his companies based on their belief in the future that he paints. To analyze TSLA correctly, we need to stop taking Musk's predictions at face value and instead assume a more sober, realistic future.

Tesla's Current and Future Revenues

Let's start with visualizing how Tesla's sales have changed between 2023 and 2025:

Energy Storage and Services

You can see that the growth in the Energy Storage and Services segments has more or less replaced the decline in the Automotive segment. Tesla's energy storage products, like "Megapack" are in extremely high demand. Megapacks are going to be an important aspect of the AI infrastructure buildout since data centers have a huge demand for energy storage. Ironically, this segment of the business seems to get much less attention from both the public and Musk himself. Musk only mentioned the Megapack product one time in the Q4 2025 earnings call. But for me, this product is one of the company's biggest opportunities: it's already a viable, selling product with a big backlog. I assume a 30% CAGR in this segment through 2030 later in the article when valuing the stock.

Additionally, I think the "Services" segment can grow significantly in the next few years. The bulk of this segment is revenues from the Supercharger network, which can now be used by certain non-Tesla vehicles. Tesla has ensured that it owns the largest network of charging stations as more and more cars are electric. This segment also includes Full Self-Driving, or FSD, subscriptions and potential licensing of the FSD system to other automakers in the future.

While I don't believe it's likely that companies like Ford (F) or General Motors (GM) will license Tesla's FSD in the near future - Ford and GM's own driver assistance programs take the #1 and #2 spots on Consumer Reports rankings - I do factor significant growth in this segment into my valuation model. That growth I believe will primarily result from more FSD subscriptions in Tesla's own vehicles and more revenue from the Supercharger network as EV ownership increases.

Looking toward potential future revenues, Tesla’s current valuation is premised on two specific outcomes in my view: autonomous robotaxis at scale and ubiquitous “Optimus” robots. We should consider what the true plausibility and likely timelines of these products actually are.

Autonomous Robotaxis

Although Musk has been promising level 5 autonomy for many years now, the actual rollout of autonomous driving over the next decade is likely to be fairly slow. The systems need to prove their capabilities in different cities and terrains. Forbes estimates 900,000 robotaxis in operation by 2035 with a market size of $100 billion. 250,000 of those vehicles are projected to be operating in the United States while 550,000 are expected to be operating in China. If that projection is correct, then the market for Tesla’s robotaxis over the next ten years is not that big, and not nearly big enough to justify a $1.5 trillion valuation today, given the company is in competition with Waymo (GOOG), Zoox (AMZN), and others.

To illustrate the degree to which fantastical expectations are attached to Tesla’s autonomous driving future, I want to look at Ark Invest’s (ARKK) “2029 Bear Outcome” - their worst-case scenario for Tesla - in which they project a $2000 share price by 2029. Their bear case is that Tesla sells 5.8 million cars for $250 billion in revenue, plus $603 billion in autonomous ride-hailing revenue.

To put that in context, Uber (UBER) made $52 billion in revenue in 2025, so Ark Invest is projecting that in the worst-case scenario, in three years, Tesla will be doing about 12x Uber’s revenue in ridesharing. Since autonomous rides are supposed to be cheaper, that means Ark is expecting at a minimum that Tesla robotaxis will be doing more than twelve times the number of rides that Uber did last year. I’ll leave it to readers to decide whether those projections are anchored to reality or not.

In reality, I think Tesla would do very well to match Uber’s revenues from ride sharing in the next ten years. Uber doesn’t depend on making its own vehicles. On the contrary, for Tesla, the pace of manufacturing will be a major bottleneck. Attaining enough chips for a massive self-driving fleet is likely to be a major challenge. Although Tesla has created its own chip, it still relies on the foundries (primarily TSMC and Samsung) to manufacture it. Those companies, of course, have other customers like Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), who could be higher priority than Tesla.

Let’s try to imagine a future where Tesla robotaxis match Uber’s number of trips. There are 100,000 Uber and Lyft (LYFT) drivers in Los Angeles alone, according to the New York Times. Most of those drivers can just keep the cars at their own houses. Even if you assume that Tesla can match that cadence with a fleet of 10,000 robotaxis in LA alone, the logistics and infrastructure required to support it are a daunting challenge. Multiply that challenge by 15,000 in order to match Uber’s ubiquity in cities around the world.

I also think Tesla may have made a strategic error by not incorporating LiDAR technology into their cars the way Alphabet's (GOOGL) Waymo has. While LiDAR was expensive at first, the costs have decreased dramatically and are likely to continue decreasing. Musk also questioned whether LiDAR was really necessary, saying

“Humans drive with eyes & biological neural nets, so makes sense that cameras and silicon neural nets are the only way to achieve generalized solution to self-driving.”

But I don’t think that actually makes much sense. We humans are frequently being tricked by optical illusions and misperceptions, and cameras are not as good as eyes. Even with our ability to reason and understand the world we see, we still make plenty of mistakes while driving. We also do use other senses while driving, albeit less frequently. You might hear a siren coming from behind you or smell your engine burning. That extra, non-optical data can improve decision-making even for humans.

I don’t see why the same principle wouldn’t apply to autonomous vehicles. More comprehensive data can allow better decisions and fewer mistakes, in my view. Personally, if I’m going to trust a machine with my life, I’d rather be in one that can literally see through walls than one that can’t.

Optimus Robot

Tesla currently has about half the revenue of General Motors (GM) and Ford (F), but about 21 times the market cap of GM and nearly 27 times the market cap of Ford. If either of those companies came out and said they were shutting down the F-150 or Silverado factories in order to start making humanoid robots, I expect the stocks would tank. But TSLA’s massive premium is currently premised on exactly that.

Digging into the numbers further, Musk said their aim is to produce 1 million Optimus robots per year:

But we're going to take the Model S and X production space in our Fremont factory and convert that into an Optimus factory, which will -- with the long-term goal of having 1 million units a year of Optimus robots in the current S, X space in Fremont. So that is slightly sad, but it is time to bring the S, X program to an end and shift really -- it's part of our overall shift to an autonomous future. As my profile picture on X said for a few months there, the future is autonomous.

Taking that number at face value, and based on a proposed selling price of Optimus of $20,000, that would generate $20 billion per year in revenue, if all million of the robots could be sold. And Musk admits this is a colossal undertaking of manufacturing:

So that means the normal S-curve of manufacturing ramp will be longer for Optimus than it is for products that have at least some portion of an existing supply chain. Like when everything is new, the production rate will be proportionate to the least lucky, least confident part of the entire supply chain. And if there's 10,000 things that need to go right, it's -- it only takes one to be slow to lag that. But -- so it will be sort of a stretched out S-curve. But I'm confident that we'll get to 1 million units a year of -- in Fremont of Optimus 3.

For the sake of argument, I’ll assume all 10,000 things go right and Tesla hits this cadence of Optimus production by 2030 and then sells all the units they actually produce. That will then provide the company an additional $20 billion in annual revenue, assuming a selling price of $20,000. I will bake that assumption into TSLA’s value, although I am unconvinced that Optimus will be adopted at anywhere near this kind of scale in the near future.

In the Q4 2025 earnings call, Musk was asked how many Optimus robots are currently “deployed in Tesla factories and performing production tasks.” Musk answered saying essentially none:

Yes. I mean we're still very much at the early stages of Optimus. It's an R&D -- it's still in the R&D phase. So we have had Optimus do some basic tasks in the factory. But as we iterate our new versions of Optimus, we deprecate the old versions. And so it's not -- I wouldn't say it's like -- it's not in usage in our factories in a material way.

I think keeping an eye on this going forward will be a good benchmark for Optimus’ capabilities. When Tesla and SpaceX (SPACE) start using Optimus at scale, that will be a good indication that the robot has true potential. But until then, there is still no proof of concept. Whether companies and households would even elect to deploy these robots in their factories or households is an open question.

Creating a humanoid robot strikes me as reinventing the wheel. I think useful robotics are likely to be more specialized. Japanese and Korean companies have decades of experience in this space, and domestic competitors like Boston Dynamics (part of Hyundai) and the company that seems to have its tentacles in every area of technology, Alphabet, are likely well ahead of Tesla as well. Even if Optimus matches the expectations that Musk has set for it, it’s not clear that a company manufacturing robots should be valued at any higher premium than a company manufacturing vehicles. What is it about a robotics company that should make it worth 10x its sales, while automakers are traditionally worth about 1x their sales?

Valuation

In December 2016, TSLA had $3.24 revenue per share with a share price of about $14.25. Today, Tesla generates $29.40 in revenue per share with a share price of $417. So on a price to sales basis it looks three times more expensive than it did as essentially a start up. In my valuation model for TSLA, I will estimate 2030 revenues and then apply an 8x equity value to sales ratio, in line with software companies like Alphabet, Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta Platforms (META), even though Tesla will likely still primarily be a hardware maker. Even being very generous with revenue estimates and the multiple, I still arrive at a fair value per share for TSLA of $301.

Estimate of Tesla 2030 Revenues

  • $50 billion robotaxis
  • $46 billion energy storage
  • $100 billion vehicle sales
  • $20 billion Optimus robots
  • $20 billion Services/Other.

These assumptions are based on Tesla matching the size of Uber in the ridesharing industry and growing energy storage sales at a 30% CAGR, increasing vehicle sales significantly despite the trend being in the other direction and the company using the Fremont factory to make robots instead of cars, and the Optimus robot selling a million units per year. That gets us to $236 billion in revenues. Applying an 8x price-to-sales ratio gets us a $1.9 trillion fair equity value in 2030. I assume that the company will continue with share dilution again to fund either CapEx or executive compensation, so I use a figure of 3.8 billion shares outstanding. That gives a fair equity value of $500 in 2030.

For required return on equity, I use a CAPM model with a beta of 1.9, equity risk premium of 5%, and risk free rate of 4%. That gives a required return on equity of 13.5%. Discounting the $500 share price in 2030 back to present (4 years) gives a fair value per share today of $301.

I want to reiterate that this all assumes flawless executional success over the next four to five years. That is, it represents an extremely bullish scenario. Even with such assumptions, I can’t justify the current price per share. A more baseline/bearish scenario would assume something like $80 billion in vehicle sales, $46 billion in energy storage revenue, about $10 billion from Optimus/robotaxis, $20 billion in services sales, and about a 2x price to sales ratio. That would price the company at $312 billion by 2030.

Right now with TSLA you’re paying $417 for $2 of EPS expected this year. Compare that to GOOG, where you’re currently paying $306 for $11.52 EPS expected this year. And Alphabet is arguably in the lead in every race in which it’s in competition with Tesla.

Final Thoughts

So far, Elon Musk has successfully managed to make his companies’ valuations about the future rather than the present. It’s possible that his companies will never be valued based on their present business so long as he manages to come up with new visions of the future to sell to investors. But it’s also possible that at some point he runs out of road to kick the can down. TSLA is priced as though the company is set to completely dominate in the fields in which it is striving to innovate.

But in reality, I think the company is probably trying to be too many things at once. It seems like the company is trying to be a Jack-of-all-trades without clear focus. That is even more true of Elon Musk himself - think of the number of different moonshots he's trying to pull off across multiple companies. He and his companies are spreading themselves too thin, in my opinion.

For me, there are better alternatives to invest in, like Alphabet, who in my view is a clear leader in a number of AI applications, the foundries like TSMC (TSM) and Samsung (SSNLF) who manufacture chips for Tesla and other chipmakers, key robotics suppliers like Micron (MU), LG Energy, and a number of publicly traded Chinese companies that make the parts for Optimus and other robots (investible through ETFs). For me, the risk/reward just isn't there with TSLA to buy shares right now.

data.title: Japan's Paradigm Shift
data.description:
Originally published on February 13, 2026

By Kei Okamura

A landslide electoral mandate could speed up reforms, support domestic demand and push corporate cash into higher returns - key ingredients for a more durable re-rating of Japanese risk assets.

Investors have been invited more than once in recent years to believe that “Japan is back.” Since the “lost decades” of the early 1990s through much of the 2000s, the challenge has not been a lack of potential catalysts. It has been consistency: turning intention into implementation, and implementation into a higher-quality earnings and return profile.

What could be different this time? Japan’s long-running equity discount has been anchored in three beliefs: policy intent rarely becomes durable execution, domestic demand is structurally fragile, and corporate cash stockpiling suppresses returns on equity (ROE). The landslide election win by Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s ruling Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) on Feb. 8 matters because it could challenge all three at once.

Paradigm shifts tend to happen not through a single announcement, but when the odds of sustained follow-through rise across policy, households and corporate behavior. In our view, three signals of regime change bear watching: faster policy sequencing, a more deliberate domestic-demand agenda, and a clearer pathway from governance to higher ROE.

1. Policy Throughput as a Valuation Variable

Japan has not historically been short of policy initiatives. The market’s discount has more often reflected uncertainty around delivery - delays, dilution, and loss of momentum. What may be different now - with the LDP winning a reported 316 of 465 Lower House seats (68.0%), described as the first single-party general-election supermajority since World War II - is throughput: the capacity to move budgets and reforms through the system with fewer friction points.

Two structural features matter. First, Parliament’s more influential Lower House can override the Upper House with a two-thirds majority, making control of that chamber decisive for budgets and key legislation.

Second, committee leadership shapes what bills become before they reach a final vote; the LDP’s control of key standing committees could help accelerate the agenda. Higher throughput does not guarantee better outcomes, but we believe it can reduce policy drift risk - an important input into Japan’s equity risk premium.

2. Boosting Domestic Demand Is Becoming an Overt Policy Objective

One lesson from Japan’s deflationary decades is the need to protect household purchasing power. That means strengthening the domestic channel by sustaining wage gains while limiting the kind of everyday inflation that erodes confidence. Prime Minister Takaichi has emphasized both, alongside measures intended to support household consumption.

If that balance holds, the opportunity set for investors in Japan could broaden beyond traditional favorites like big exporters and cyclical winners that benefit from currency translation. But constraints still matter. Rates and the yen remain the market’s feedback loop: tighter financial conditions can pressure domestic earnings, while currency moves can help exporters yet squeeze households through import costs.

3. ROE Is a Key Signal for Japan’s Re-rating

The most investable part of Takaichi’s agenda, in our view, is the implied funding model: mobilizing Japan Inc.’s underutilized balance sheets rather than leaning exclusively on the public balance sheet. That matters because Japan’s equity market discount has long reflected cash-rich, return-poor corporate structures.

We see this as one reason many global investors have remained underweight Japanese equities, despite the improving picture in recent years on dividend payouts and share buybacks. For example, the MSCI Japan Index trades at about 16x price to FY2027 earnings, and the MSCI Japan Small Cap Index trades at about 14x - versus about 20x for the S&P 500 (Bloomberg data as of February 9, 2026).

This is why Takaichi’s proposed revision to the Corporate Governance Code later this year is important. If it raises expectations around capital allocation transparency, we believe it can reinforce more systematic capital discipline - clearer frameworks, higher hurdle rates, and restructuring of low-return activities.

The same lens applies to the push toward investment in 17 “crisis management” industries tied to national security and economic resilience - including semiconductors, shipping, energy and autos. For investors, the test will be whether policy support and governance pressure translate into better returns on capital.

Caveats and Portfolio Implications

Markets will still anchor expectations to fiscal credibility (with Japan’s gross debt cited at a hefty 230% of GDP, even as deficits have been comparatively modest at around 0.6% of GDP), as well as the path of rates and FX, and tangible signs of policy execution. The case for a re-rating will require visible progress in real wages, consumption resilience, and corporate actions that improve capital efficiency and ROE.

At the portfolio level, these dynamics mean Japanese equities are best framed as a core allocation, not a tactical add-on. Emphasize exposure to the ROE pathway - focusing on capital discipline and the governance-driven use of corporate cash - while pairing investment-led beneficiaries with domestic-demand sensitivity. Treat rates and the yen as explicit risk variables, not background assumptions.

Bottom Line

The takeaway is straightforward: the election outcome increases the chances that reforms are delivered. Clear, timely delivery can shrink Japan’s implementation discount, broaden earnings leadership, and put more corporate cash to work to lift ROE. If these changes show up in the data, Japan’s re-rating could prove more durable than past false starts - and harder for long-term investors to ignore.
