data.title: Axalta: Underwhelming Results, An Equally Underwhelming Potential Merger
data.description:
For a company that I had thought was making progress, Axalta Coating Systems Ltd.’s (AXTA) performance since my last update has been disappointing. Weakness in the company’s served end markets certainly hasn’t helped, but I expected more from this company. More disappointing still was management’s decision to enter into what was effectively a no-premium merger with a lackluster player (Akzo Nobel (AKZOY)) where synergies could well disappoint.

Axalta shares are down about 5% since that last article, underperforming all of its peers in what has admittedly been a rougher period for specialty chemical companies due to cost inflation and weaker end-market demand.

The deal with Akzo is not yet finalized, and both shareholder and regulatory approvals are pending. At this point, I would say the likelihood of a competing bid is low, though the valuation is not prohibitive to a higher bid. I do believe multiple end markets will improve in 2026 and that there could be some upside to the combined companies, but my lack of confidence in Akzo management tamps down whatever long-term enthusiasm I might have.

A Disappointing Q4… And I Have Some Questions

Axalta reported weaker-than-expected fourth-quarter results, with misses at the revenue and earnings lines, as well as soft guidance for the upcoming year.

Revenue fell 4% as reported and more than 7% in organic terms, missing by around 1%. Gross margin weakened 70 bp to 33.4%, and overall price/mix (down 0.4%) was not helpful. Adjusted EBITDA declined 1% (margin up 60 bp to 21.6%), missing by 4%, while operating income declined 3% (margin up 10 bp to 16.5%), missing by 6%.

Performance revenue declined 10% in organic terms, with double-digit declines in Refinish and a high single-digit decline in Industrial. Segment EBITDA fell 9%, with margin down 70 bp to 22.8%.

Mobility revenue fell about 3% in organic terms, with light vehicles down low single-digits organically and commercial vehicles down low single-digits as well. EBITDA rose 20%, with margin up almost three points to 19.4%.

PPG’s (PPG) Performance Coatings business saw 3% organic growth in the quarter, with a high single-digit decline in refinish, double-digit growth in aerospace and protective & marine, and low single-digit growth in traffic solutions. PPG’s Industrial Coatings business grew 4% organically, with flat industrial sales, double-digit growth in packaging, and low single-digit growth in auto OEM.

Akzo reported low single-digit contraction in its Marine & Protective business, flat Automotive & Specialty (with strong aero), and a mid-single-digit decline in Industrial, with “weak” packaging.

I’m not surprised about the weakness in auto refinishing given market dynamics (insurance claims, pricing pressures, et al.). The auto OEM performance is more disappointing given slight production growth in the fourth quarter (up around 1% per S&P Global) and Axalta’s comparative success with Chinese LV OEMs.

The weakness in Industrial is more surprising and disappointing to me. Multiple industrials have reported better results in the fourth quarter, and a high single-digit decline is weak relative to its rivals. Some of this could be a mix (a bigger skew to appliances and so on), but it’s an area of concern going into 2026.

Merging With Akzo Is Far From Compelling

The biggest recent development for Axalta is the November announcement of what is effectively a merger of equals with Akzo Nobel. Under the terms of the deal, Axalta shareholders will get 0.6539 Akzo shares for each of their shares, and Akzo shareholders will ultimately own 55% of the business (Akzo shareholders will also be getting a special dividend before the deal closes).

With the share prices having moved since the deal was announced, Akzo’s offer currently values Axalta at about 10.9x forward EBITDA. Although that is a sizable discount to the 13x EBITDA multiple that BASF (BASFY) got for its BASF Coatings business—a business that I would argue is no better than, and possibly inferior to, Axalta—it's not unfair relative to my estimate of a fair multiple based on Axalta’s likely margins and returns.

There are some positives to this deal, including a more diverse business mix and some potential revenue synergies (maybe 100 bp–200 bp) across the business. It also answers Akzo’s desire to diverse into auto markets and Axalta’s desire to diversify away from auto.

Nevertheless, I don’t like it as a long-term option for Axalta shareholders.

Akzo has been in a seemingly never-ending restructuring mode, and the shares are down 30% over the past five years and 20% over the past decade, with poor revenue, operating income, and free cash flow performance, as well as feeble metrics like ROIC.

In contrast, despite some weakness in this latest down-cycle, Axalta can at least boast better than 6% revenue growth over the last five years, market share gains, better free cash flow generation, and better ROICs. On a longer-term basis, Axalta has been gaining share in refinish and industrial and has done a good job of penetrating the Chinese auto market with a service-oriented model that locates within OEM facilities and offers a broader range of products than its main rivals.

In addition to my concerns about the quality of Akzo management, I have questions about the cost synergies. The target of $600 million is about 18% of combined EBITDA, which is on the high end of successful industrial mergers. I believe Axalta has improved its operations to a point where there isn’t a lot of low-hanging fruit now, and I question whether Akzo management is up to the challenge of achieving above-average cost synergies with an already efficiently run operation.

I have to acknowledge that Axalta has not done particularly well in the stock market over the last 10 years, but Akzo hasn’t done that much better. I might prefer this deal if it were Axalta management running the combined entity, but as is, I don’t see this as a clear long-term value builder for Axalta shareholders—there is upside, but that has to be discounted by the probability that management will not like it, and I have my doubts there.

The Outlook

Axalta offered weak guidance for the upcoming year, with EBITDA about 2% below the Street at the midpoint. I do see some possibilities for upside, though. Industrial markets are improving, and Axalta is leveraged to attractive sub-sectors like electrical products, and the comps will get easier from here. Likewise, I expect improving comps in the refinish market, and as miles driven continue to grow, accident frequency should increase.

I’m expecting around 2% revenue growth in 2026, improving to around 3% over the next three years and longer term. As end markets recover, I believe Axalta can slowly build EBITDA margins toward the mid-20%s, and I expect this to drive low double-digit free cash flow margins and solid mid-single-digit free cash flow growth over the long term.

Axalta looks undervalued on both a discounted cash flow and margin/return-driven EV/EBITDA approach. The former suggests a high single-digit long-term annualized potential return, while an 11x multiple on EBITDA would support a fair value near $47.50.

Valuation is now driven at least in part by the value of the pending Akzo merger. As much as I don’t like the deal, at this point the completion of the deal would offer meaningful upside, with an imputed value of over $46/share for Axalta.

The Bottom Line

I think Axalta is worth considering as long as there’s such a gap between Axalta’s share price and the imputed deal value. There are risks that the deal won’t go forward, but I think Axalta’s underlying standalone value offers some downside protection. I wouldn’t be all that interested in owning the new Akzo shares long term, but so long as the deal offers share price upside, this is a name worth considering, and particularly so given that end markets should start recovering as 2026 moves on.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: January CPI Report: Unusually Cooler Than Normal
data.description:
Originally published on February 13, 2026

By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist; Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist; and Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

Consumer prices in January came in softer than expected, with the annual headline rate easing to 2.4%, from 2.7% previously. Core inflation, which excludes food and energy, rose 2.5% year-over-year, slightly below last month’s reading. Though this month’s report is typically noisy amid seasonal effects, the lack of any upward surprise signals that price pressures remain contained.

While inflation remains above 2%, the report is reassuring for markets as it signals tariff pass-throughs have remained modest so far. The broader trend of cooling price pressures, especially as shelter inflation continues to normalize, should also ease concerns about renewed inflation momentum, suggesting the Federal Reserve’s 2% target is within reach.

Report details

  • Headline inflation rose 0.2% from the prior month, lower than expected, bringing the annual rate to 2.4%, from 2.7% prior. Core inflation, which excludes food and energy, rose 0.3%, as expected, bringing the annual rate to 2.5%, the slowest pace since March 2021. While January inflation reports tend to be noisy, given that companies typically raise prices at the start of the year, this cycle’s tariff pass-through effects add an additional layer of complexity. Encouragingly, the lack of any real upward surprise suggests the trajectory of cooling price pressures remains intact.
  • The highly volatile energy category declined 1.5% in the month, weighed down by a significant drop in gasoline prices, which more than offset the rise in natural gas prices amid a cold snap in most parts of the country. This dynamic was similarly evident in the reversal in strength in restaurant prices, as colder weather likely curtailed dining out activity. Together with an easing in the pace of increase in grocery prices, overall food inflation appeared to normalize following last month’s spike.
  • Core inflation continues to be driven by services prices and shelter costs, which increased 0.2% in the period. Nevertheless, owners’ equivalent rent, a major component, has continued to steadily decelerate. Prices of discretionary services categories also rose, particularly airfares, which increased at the fastest pace since May 2022, suggesting robust demand for travel.
  • Tariff-sensitive goods categories showed limited upward pressure. The increase in apparel and household furnishings prices remained subdued. Similarly, the increase in vehicle prices was restrained, with used cars and truck prices declining meaningfully for the second month in a row. Meanwhile, electronics and personal care products did see price increases. Overall, while there may still be some lingering effects of tariff pass-through, barring additional new tariffs, this effect should begin to fade by mid-year as we pass the initial surge in tariff rates from a year ago.
  • The Fed's preferred supercore inflation measure increased by 0.6% in the period, keeping the annual rate flat at 2.7% from the prior month. The supercore inflation measure, which excludes shelter from core services and is primarily driven by wage costs, has not yet been an issue given the ongoing softening in labor demand.

Policy outlook

Overall, as inflation came in as expected, markets are likely breathing a sigh of relief as price pressures remain contained despite a very strong labor market report earlier in the week and amid persistent concerns over further tariff pass-through.

With core inflation now at almost a four-year low and the Fed’s 2% target within reach, this is a reassuring data point. Nevertheless, this falls short of justifying near-term rate cuts. Continued labor market resilience gives policymakers cover to stay on hold, while further disinflation in the second half of the year - as tariff effects fade - should reopen the door to further easing.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: American Tower: Steadily Growing Long-Term Demand Drivers, Shares Attractive
data.description:
American Tower Corporation (AMT) is a real estate investment trust (“REIT”) that owns, operates, and develops multitenant communications real estate. This includes the tower structure, land parcel, and the backup power. In essence, their property holdings are critical components of the wireless communications networks used by millions across the U.S.

Shares in the stock are often one of the largest holdings by real estate ETFs offered by various brokerages. Within the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), for example, the stock is a top 5 holding, representing nearly 5% of the fund.

At present, the stock is trading around the mid-point of its 52-week range. For investors seeking an individualized play within the real estate sector, I believe AMT is one stock that should be an anchor holding. At current prices, I believe investors would be afforded a reasonable entry point into a durable company with a positive operational outlook.

AMT Stock Key Metrics

One aspect of AMT's business model is the long-term nature of the leases. Initial terms are generally 5 to 10 years, and embedded are multiple renewal terms. In addition, the leases include annual escalators averaging 3%. Tenant lock-in over this term is supplemented by relatively low capex requirements for AMT.

This business model enables AMT to generate a stable and recurring revenue stream, which in turn translates to consistent growth in EBITDA and adjusted funds from operations (“AFFO”).

A case in point is that over the past 10 years from 2014 to 2024, AMT has grown segment revenue and adjusted EBITDA at a compound growth rate (“CAGR”) of about 10%. These ultimately have translated to significant overall shareholder returns, including a 17% CAGR in its dividend/share since 2014.

It shouldn't be a surprise, then, that Wall Street remains bullish overall on the stock, despite recent downgrades at the end of 2025 from Barclays. With regards to a price target, analysts covering the stock see shares fairly valued at around the $220/share mark. This would represent almost 15% upside potential from current trading values.

I believe AMT can reach this mark, especially given AMT’s favorable demand drivers. In its last investor presentation, AMT presented facts surrounding global mobile data usage over the next several years. The data was striking, in my view. In the U.S., usage is expected to grow about 12% CAGR from 2025 through 2030. In Europe, it's about 13% over the same period.

The continued double-digit percentage rise should continue to serve as leverage for AMT in both its lease rates and renewals as well as locking in new contracts. While AMT’s Q4 results are pending at the time of this article, Q3 results showed that AMT’s fundamentals and outlook remain positive, in my view.

AMT Operating Performance

AMT’s most recently completed quarter was Q3, and this was released at the end of October 2025. At that time, I viewed the results as an expectation beating quarter that was paired with positive forward guidance.

First, topline revenues during the period increased nearly 8% YOY, driven by steady mid-single-digit organic growth, strong U.S. services activity, and double-digit growth at CoreSite.

In addition, property revenue rose about 6%, while consolidated organic tenant billings growth came in at 5%. This is exactly the kind of steady, predictable performance investors expect from this business model.

More to the bottom line, adjusted EBITDA also climbed nearly 8%, supported not just by revenue growth but by disciplined cost management. In fact, cash margins expanded modestly, a continuing trend from past quarters.

The management team also highlighted roughly 300 basis points of EBITDA margin expansion since 2020. That operating leverage is important, particularly in a higher-rate environment, in my view.

Most notably, AFFO/share grew approximately 10% YOY. That’s strong bottom-line execution, especially for a company already operating at scale. Given these results, the guidance was confident enough to include a trifecta raise to full-year revenue, EBITDA, and AFFO.

Between the continued 5% organic tenant billings growth, 14%+ data center revenue growth, and leverage now below 5x, Q3 supported what I would view as the base case for AMT. That is durable demand and steady AFFO/share growth.

What Is The Outlook For AMT Stock?

As mentioned, Q4 included raised full-year guidance across revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and AFFO. At the midpoint, AMT now expects roughly 7% growth in attributable AFFO/share as adjusted, or closer to 9% when excluding FX and financing impacts.

Organic tenant billings growth is expected to remain steady at about 5%, with data center revenue expected to grow approximately 13% for the year. That is not hypergrowth, but it is consistent, visible, and supported by strong mobile data consumption trends globally, in my view.

AMT is set to report Q4 results pre-market on February 24, and based on the raised outlook and its YTD performance, I believe the company is well positioned to meet expectations.

Ahead of the release, AMT has faced some downgrades. At the end of December, for example, analysts at Barclays downgraded AMT to "equal weight," citing concerns around potential EchoStar-related rent collection risks and limited near-term re-rating potential.

The price target was also trimmed to $200/share. While that headline created some noise, the broader analyst community still sees fair value closer to $220, implying even greater upside from current levels.

In my view, the downgrade reflects caution around a specific tenant situation rather than deterioration in AMT’s core operating trends. I ultimately believe the long-term demand drivers remain firmly intact.

Is AMT Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I like AMT at current levels, and I see it as a Buy for investors seeking positioning in the real estate sector.

The stock is trading around the midpoint of its 52-week range, even as the company continues to deliver mid-to-high single-digit organic growth and double-digit AFFO/share growth. Given the results, I believe investors are today being offered a reasonable entry point into a business with long-term, visible demand drivers that are not cyclical in nature.

In terms of demand-side factors, mobile data usage continues to grow at a double-digit pace globally. In the U.S. alone, consumption has been increasing at an extraordinary rate, and management expects usage to continue compounding through the end of the decade.

More data usage ultimately requires more network capacity, more densification, and more equipment on towers. That directly benefits AMT’s leasing activity and renewal economics. Add to that the continued momentum in CoreSite and early AI-related workloads, and the runway appears long, in my view.

Yes, there are near-term headlines around tenant disputes and downgrades. But I believe the core operating engine, positive organic tenant billings growth, expanding margins, and disciplined capital allocation, remains intact.

For investors seeking a durable REIT with steady AFFO growth and a long-term infrastructure tailwind, I believe AMT deserves to be an anchor holding at today’s price.

data.title: Nvidia And The Architecture Of The Technological Dollar
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), which is up only about 3% since my last analysis in December 2025, might look stuck in consolidation. However, the company is emerging as the primary provider of the structural architecture behind a latent dollar restrengthening regime. Ultimately, this reemergence of structural unipolarity will be driven by autonomous technologies driving higher GDP growth. Of course, this cannot happen without Nvidia.

This technological age will drive Nvidia's primacy in three ways. As AI productivity diffuses into core workflows, financial rails become more programmable, and USD network effects reconsolidate dominant-currency pricing and reserve status, Nvidia will likely be as ubiquitous as water. The cycle of growth we are already witnessing the birth of is as follows:

Compute > AI Diffusion > Productivity > GDP Growth > Fiscal Optics > USD Asset Demand

It's very simple, and it is surprising that sovereign states have been so quick to attack the world order in a temporary pocket of dollar weakness. Nvidia monetizes the conversion of labor and software spend into intelligent compute, and when that conversion becomes economy-wide and deeply penetrated, it will raise potential growth and improve debt-to-GDP via the denominator. Of course, debt reduction remains policy-driven, but consensus is so strong on the point of debt reduction necessity right now that the next decade looks like a clear path to victory.

Perhaps the greatest structural risk is not the debt-to-GDP itself, but the fear of geopolitical crisis in the interim driving irrational acts of war that could delay or destabilize markets in the interim. Patience, restraint, and technological advance appear to be all that is necessary for stability in the world order and its markets to return.

New-Era Financial Architecture

USD tokenization, or the so-called "digital dollar," has a high chance of underpinning a next-generation monetary and financial system. While this may presently seem distant, truthfully it is already being implemented, as evidenced by the Bank for International Settlements. Scalable tokenized rails across central bank reserves, commercial bank money, and government bonds imply heavier real-time advanced compute needs, likely augmented by agentic AI, which Nvidia will be the primary structural base of.

If AI more broadly increases the GDP growth of America first, and its allies second, and its direct competitors third, then USD invoicing and pricing can persist as a stable equilibrium in world trade. At that juncture, upgrades in U.S.-based financial plumbing through mechanisms such as tokenization will likely disproportionately reinforce demand for dollar-denominated collateral and settlement risks.

Bear in mind that we are already seeing structural evidence of this thesis playing out, so it is not wishful thinking or speculation. Customer service deployments show about a 14% productivity lift from genAI assistance, coding field experiments show more than 50% higher code output for junior staff, and controlled Copilot experiments are showing about a 55.8% faster task completion. Of course, higher efficiency equals higher demand for the source of such efficiency gains, which at the fundamental level of raw compute is Nvidia. Anecdotally, I am hearing reports and seeing evidence of people completing 4x the amount of work they used to without AI, at the high end of the curve for advanced AI adoption. I expect eventually this will become the norm.

Overcoming Constraints

In the near term, power and infrastructure will be more constrained than chip supply. The IEA estimates that around 20% of data center projects could be at risk of delays absent grid fixes, and it notes that transmission buildouts can take between four and eight years. Nvidia's own 10-Q warns that customers may delay adopting new architectures if their data center infrastructure isn't ready, so even if demand is strong due to accruing efficiency benefits across the macroeconomy from AI adoption, Nvidia's near-term revenue slope may be capped and volatility may emerge temporarily as a result. I would see this as a buying opportunity for NVDA stock, as I forecast expectations will then normalize, energy access will be innovated on and strengthened across the free world, and Nvidia will have a following bull market, "phase 2," in the AI supercycle.

The mitigation lever to this energy constraint that Nvidia has control over is its deliverance of performance per watt. Nvidia has cited SemiAnalysis InferenceMAX benchmarks where already Blackwell has delivered 10x throughput per megawatt versus the prior generation. That said, there is clearly an ongoing opportunity here for refined chip architectures, and it's clear why Nvidia recently licensed Groq's inference tech and hired key Groq personnel.

Geopolitical Risks

Multipolar war arriving early is a potential medium-term outcome that could materially fragment the trajectory of AI productivity diffusing into the Western economies and improving U.S. fiscal optics. For Nvidia, among most other tech companies, the Taiwan risk is existential for near-term cash flows. Nvidia is dependent on TSMC (TSM) in Taiwan to manufacture its semiconductors. CSIS estimates about $2.45T of goods, over one-fifth of global maritime trade, transited the Taiwan Strait. Therefore, disruption becomes a trade, commodity, and financing shock that could probably freeze the data center buildout cycle. Albeit, this would be a Chinese kamikaze.

Nonetheless, GMFUS states a major war over Taiwan would likely separate a smaller global economy into three blocs, which would force NVDA growth forecasts to be bloc-constrained. At least temporarily, technological dollar stabilization would become a regional outcome rather than a global one. Yet meanwhile, China would end up as an isolated, heavily sanctioned state in a degenerated multipolar world order that likely would not endure.

At present, BIS research on international debt securities finds that the dollar share in 2024 is roughly equivalent to its share in 2000, while the world lacks a viable replacement. This clearly supports the continued depth of U.S. capital markets while AI infrastructure is being financed. China may have renminbi-denominated transaction networks and cross-border payment projects that it is designing with Western sanctions in mind, but this will never realistically be a global financial anchor in the 21st century and rather acts as a geoeconomic backstop for the CCP.

Valuation

Firstly, we must address the elephant in the room. The S&P 500 is de facto overvalued at the time of this writing without AI-driven narrative sentiment holding up a presently negative earnings yield spread. Albeit, such narrative sustenance is possible, because if the margin and GDP growth benefits are as strong as the emerging Nvidia-anchored autonomous economy suggests, the S&P 500 may in fact actually be cheap. That said, it is far too risky to bet the farm on a structural revolution in stock market valuation mechanics. To be clear, I am not saying that won't happen, though.

Nvidia specifically has a strong few years of consensus growth ahead of it, but I am more concerned about the calendar year 2028, which is Nvidia's fiscal 2029. My current premonition, based on data findings from the energy sector discussed above, is that capex spending may curtail temporarily due to bottlenecks in calendar year 2028. As markets price about a year in advance, 2027 appears as a plausible correction window. I cannot be sure of this, but it appears logical given the present valuation excess, the reality of finite Earth-bound energy, and the compressing and even negative free cash flow emerging amongst hyperscalers.

As a result, my current base-case forecast looks as follows. I find it plausible that NVDA will test all-time highs slightly and moderately by the end of calendar year 2026, before a meaningful correction and buying window emerges before phase 2 of the AI supercycle begins.

Conclusion: Hold

Those who believe that multipolarity will define geopolitics over the next turn of history may be in for an awakening. The Nvidia-anchored autonomous world order is already emerging, but it has not yet bloomed. This spud, tended to carefully by an elite who are focused on energy and intelligence and not concerned with the binary spoils and travesties of war, has the potential to revitalize the dollar in a new technological form and age. There is little reason to be bearish on Nvidia long-term, but instead, one has to place the stock price in perspective with broader macro valuations and real energy constraints to position capital with prudence, foresight, and respect for the global order at hand.

Addendum: Q4 Preview

Nvidia reports Q4 FY26 results on Wednesday, Feb 25, after the market closes. I would be particularly interested to hear CEO Jensen Huang's comments on what his outlook is on the energy environment, though I expect his wording to be conservative given how impactful his statements are on stock sentiment.

I understand how bullish Huang and the company are on the long-term hyperscaling trajectory, but I am hoping for comments in the earnings call about the fiscal condition of its major customers and the reliability of compounding growth for NVDA in the medium term in the face of emerging compressing and even negative free cash flow for these customers. Ultimately, we can expect this earnings print to be positive, and I have a feeling that management will not let any near-term weaknesses leak, instead focusing on the more positive, optimistic, and bullish long horizon of intelligent compute dominance that Nvidia commands.

data.title: Microsoft's Azure Reacceleration Could Shock Wall Street
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), despite being on the edge of AI development and having multiple layers of future monetization from this novel tech, has sold off the most since the beginning of 2026, and its negative momentum "beats" Amazon (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA), Nvidia (NVDA), and actually all top-7 tech firms, let alone the S&P 500 index.

The market seems to be punishing MSFT for the lack of immediate return on invested capital. CAPEX is rising, but they aren't converting a sufficient share of M365 commercial seats into paid Copilot users, which is one of the reasons the market is concerned about the firm's future standing in the AI race. But I think it's premature to judge MSFT's future success based solely on a few metrics. The catalysts that I see ahead seem to give MSFT a comfortable upside potential, in my opinion, so the YTD selloff has given long-term investors a nice opportunity to load up while the stock is relatively cheap.

Catalysts 2026-2028: Agents, Silicon, And Quantum Supremacy

The main catalyst that I see for Microsoft is its transition toward agentic AI and the computing infrastructure around of it. Today's chatbots are mainly used for data collection and representation, and oftentimes, when one tries to run a reasoning-based research task, these bots fail to perform at a sufficient quality. However, the current level of AI development can already make agents work at a good level - agents can manage workflows (which doesn't require much "comprehension" from them) or perform standard coding tasks. According to Grand View Research's study, the AI agents market is expected to grow from $7.63 billion (as of 2025) up to $183 billion by 2033. And Microsoft seems to be one of the potential winners of this market due to things like the OpenClaw open-source platform. Here's how another Seeking Alpha author, Gene Chan, CFA, described this product's capabilities in his article:

You interact with OpenClaw just like you could with a human assistant—converse with it via chat apps on your phone, such as WhatsApp or Telegram.

You describe in plain English (or another human language) what you want, when you want it, and how you want it.

The power of OpenClaw comes from the fact that it is open-sourced, can be hosted on the cloud or locally, and can basically connect to any services you give it access to:

Your Google Mail, Amazon account, travel agent, bank account, brokerage, and Seeking Alpha account... you get the idea.

This magical combination of persistence and flexibility layered on top of the latest LLM for core intelligence made OpenClaw the first autonomous agent to really live up to its role.

(Taken from Gene Chan, CFA's article)

Gene Chan wrote that Microsoft can basically evolve Copilot into an "Enterprise OpenClaw" by granting agents unique identities in the enterprise LDAP (similar to human employees). And it can then provide them with human-like access to corporate environments managed via established Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) permissions. It's unlikely the area of the market's interest right now; otherwise, the stock wouldn't have tanked on higher CAPEX projections. But if we recall the level of so-called "under-utilization" of MSFT's 450 million-seat M365 commercial base, and if we assume that this agentic tech can increase conversions thanks to its cost-effectiveness, I think MSFT's AI opportunity has only become bigger over the past few months.

Another catalyst that makes MSFT a better investment now compared to, say, 2024, is the firm's progress on hardware stack verticalization. In other words, Microsoft has launched the Maia 200 AI accelerator, which was built on TSMC's (TSM) 3nm process with 100 billion transistors, and this accelerator should have 3-4x performance of Amazon's Trainium3 chip (and outpace Google's (GOOG) TPU v7 as well).

If this silicon can save up to 30% in per-dollar performance for Microsoft (vs. GPUs), the company decouples its inference costs from Nvidia's (NVDA) pricing power, which is a great feature if we keep in mind that inference workloads are going to outweigh training over time (in some occurrences, it's already happening).

Microsoft also introduced the Cobalt 200 CPU that offers up to 50% "higher performance compared to their first custom-built processor for cloud-native workloads", per the management's commentaries on the earnings call. It's very important for Azure's profitability over time.

By the way, Azure (with other cloud services revenues combined) went up by 39% YoY in Q2, which was only 1% lower vs. Q1. The platform is capacity-constrained, based on commentaries, so it basically means that once Microsoft adds additional capacity and it optimizes performance with the above-discussed tech stack, Azure is likely to surprise the market to the upside, in my opinion.

Another catalyst that the market seems to ignore for now comes from the Majorana 1 chip that Microsoft introduced last year, which can lead to the industrialization of quantum computing. They're using breakthrough topoconductor materials to create qubits (the basic unit of information used to encode data in quantum computing, per IBM's (IBM) definition) that are digitally controlled via voltage pulses. We can say that with Majorana, MSFT can scale to 1 million qubits "on a single chip that fits in the palm of a hand", as Andy Pardoe put it in his article.

Unlike what NVDA's CEO, Jensen Huang, said about quantum computing tech's development timeline, MSFT's representatives noted that their QC will be within years (not decades). If so, Microsoft can industrialize quantum computing ahead of others and open up a completely new TAM layer for itself over the next few years, in my understanding.

Triggers For MSFT's Bullish Repricing

First, I expect Azure revenue to beat after-Q2 expectations that were likely hammered after the earnings release. Seeking Alpha data clearly shows that over the past 30 days, the analysts' revenue consensus slightly dropped for the upcoming 2 quarters.

The firm is going to spend more on cloud and AI needs, and we've seen how quickly Azure can grow the top line in fiscal 2026 - with more spending ahead, I think Azure's growth will only accelerate. Especially so as MSFT keeps adding supply capacity (from massive new sites like Fairwater). So, there's a high chance that Azure will come back to 40%+ growth in Q3-Q4, and if it does, the company should beat the consolidated revenue growth consensus.

Second, the restructuring of OpenAI liability can become a relief factor if OpenAI renegotiates its commitments or diversifies its infrastructure to other clouds. It sounds a bit counterintuitive - why would that decoupling be a relief for MSFT? But the market seems to be viewing the $250 billion commitment from OpenAI as a concentration risk, so ironically, this kind of decoupling should be accretive to Microsoft, allowing it to step off the low-margin compute recycling and reallocate those funds into high-ROIC ventures like Copilot and agentic services that I described above.

Third, we don't have to wait for the next quarterly release to see a potential bounce back above $500 per share because there's a technical analysis setup forming. The MACD levels are in the "buy" zone, and the RSI is the lowest it's been since April 2025, when MSFT dropped and then strongly rebounded. The odds for another leg upward look quite high to me.

Fourth, MSFT's valuation levels are also supportive for an eventual recovery. When the stock was trading this cheap on a P/E basis over the past few years, investors then saw a strong move to the upside:

MSFT is the 2nd cheapest company in the top-7 group based on the forward GAAP P/E ratio, and it's the 3rd cheapest on the forward EV/EBITDA basis, according to Seeking Alpha.

I think there will be bullish revisions ahead for Microsoft's EPS annual figures - assuming 5% on top of FY2027 EPS projections doesn't look like something unrealistic to me. So, instead of the current FY2027 $18.85 EPS, I get ~$19.79/share in earnings for the year. After putting 25-27x on that corrected EPS estimation, I get a price target of $514.6 per share, which is ~28% above today's stock price. And that PT of mine looks very conservative when we compare it to what Wall Street's consensus predicts:

Risks To My Views

While I noted multiple times throughout the article that I believe MSFT's CAPEX should lead to better Azure growth ahead, the company is investing quite aggressively now, and there's a major risk that we won't see any long-term value from those investments. The management noted that 2/3 of the current spend is directed toward GPUs and CPUs, which have a relatively short-lived nature - if MSFT faces physical and functional obsolescence of AI hardware sooner than I expect, the firm's margins will drop. And if Copilot subscription growth doesn't scale fast enough, there will be risks to the top line, and I believe it will only make the situation worse.

Also, MSFT may not be as undervalued as I view it. The stock might keep dropping lower, hitting 18-20x forward P/E at some point. If you recall, when Google was on sale in early 2025, its GAAP P/E was reaching 17x at one point. So, if we assume that MSFT follows a similar price action path, we might see the stock touching low-$300/share at some point.

Conclusion

From what I see, MSFT is doing everything right - without CAPEX increases, they could lose the AI race to others, so it's a necessity. So far, the market's concerns regarding the lack of ROIC look overblown to me. There are plenty of monetization levers ahead for Microsoft, in my opinion, that are currently ignored by the market. The likely combination of a slight multiple expansion with a single round of positive forward EPS consensus adjustment points to an undervaluation of about 28%. Wall Streeters have an average price target at 48%+, so MSFT is starting to look like a steal after its YTD selloff.
